title: How to make a score (using a score)
description: The most meta score of all: a score that explains how to make scores. If you're reading this, you're already inside one. There's no exit.

scores.user: pix-intrigued Interaction scores? Like music notation but for interfaces? This is getting philosophical.
scores.dialogue: pix-desktop Opens PiX in the browser. The Score Library loads with pre-loaded examples.
scores.system: pix-database Loads scores from browser's IndexedDB. Shows seeded examples.
scores.note: Meta fact: this very score you're reading was loaded exactly like this.

scores.user: pix-think I want to create a new score. I see a 'New Score' button. Nice, no bureaucracy here.
scores.dialogue: pix-button Click 'New Score'. A modal appears with two layout options.

scores.user: pix-face 'Interaction Score' (3 layers) or 'Service Blueprint' (5 layers). Let's start simple.
scores.dialogue: pix-select Modal: Interaction Score (User, Dialogue, System) vs Service Blueprint (adds Environment and Supporting Processes).
scores.note: 3 layers work for most cases. Use 5 when you need to document the full backstage.

scores.user: pix-smiling An empty grid with 3 rows and 1 column. Like a blank page but less intimidating.
scores.dialogue: pix-edit Editable fields: score title, description. Grid with 3 layers. Toolbar on top.
scores.system: pix-process Auto-save every 500ms. No save button because you don't need one.
scores.note: First: name your score and describe who the user is and what they're trying to do.

scores.user: pix-think I click on a cell and type 'pix-' to see what happens...
scores.dialogue: pix-autocomplete A pixogram menu appears! 160 icons organized by layer. User layer: emotions. Dialogue: UI elements. System: processes.
scores.system: pix-update Detects current layer and suggests relevant icons. The prefix renders as an SVG icon.
scores.note: Format: 'pix-think Is this the right page?' → renders as icon + text. This is the basic unit of a score.

scores.user: pix-surprised The user layer icons are little faces! Like the ones you're seeing right now in this row.
scores.dialogue: pix-tap Select an icon, add descriptive text after it. Press TAB to jump to the next cell.
scores.note: Yes, this score uses the same icons it's teaching you to use. Very meta.

scores.user: pix-person USER: what the person thinks, feels, expects, or wonders.
scores.dialogue: pix-dialogue DIALOGUE: what they see on screen and the actions they take (clicks, inputs, navigation).
scores.system: pix-process SYSTEM: what happens behind the scenes (APIs, databases, validations).
scores.note: Vertical = simultaneous. Each column = a moment in time. Read left to right, like music.

scores.user: pix-happy Cells can be empty. Not everything happens in every layer at the same time.
scores.dialogue: pix-button Click '+' to add a new step (column). Use step titles to mark different screens.
scores.system: pix-update The grid grows. Each new step is a 'tick' of the system.
scores.note: How long should a score be? As long as you need. This one has 10 steps and it's not complaining.

scores.user: pix-think Done. Now I want to share it with my team.
scores.dialogue: pix-down Export button in the toolbar. Formats: SVG (vector), PDF (vector), PNG (raster), JSON (re-importable).
scores.system: pix-process Generates the file and downloads it. JSON can be re-imported in another PiX session.
scores.note: SVG and PDF are great for reports. JSON to keep editing. PNG for a quick preview.

scores.user: pix-surprised So... the score I just read was made with the tool it just taught me to use?
scores.dialogue: pix-page Yes. This score is a real example stored as JSON in the examples/ folder of the repository.
scores.system: pix-merge PiX is open source. Repository on GitHub: github.com/eadpucv/pix
scores.note: Welcome to recursion. If you understood this score, you already know how to make scores.
